{"step_by_step_thinking": "The query asks whether frozen section alters surgical management of multinodular thyroid disease. Reviewing the documents, there is no direct mention of frozen section being used to alter surgical management in multinodular thyroid disease. However, Document [10] states that frozen section should not be requested in clinically benign nodules, and it is a common practice in cases of suspected papillary thyroid carcinoma to guide lymphadenectomy. Document [14] and [19] emphasize that a final diagnosis of follicular adenoma or thyroid adenoma is made only after ruling out capsular or vascular invasion through histological examination after thyroidectomy. This implies that definitive diagnosis and surgical decision-making rely on final histopathology, not frozen sections. Frozen sections are primarily used in parathyroid surgery (Document [3], [18]) to confirm tissue type, not to alter management in multinodular goiter or benign nodular disease. There is no evidence in the documents that frozen section changes the decision between lobectomy and total thyroidectomy in multinodular thyroid disease. Therefore, based on the provided information, frozen section does not alter surgical management in multinodular thyroid disease.", "answer_choice": "B"}
